thread.title: The Nicaraguan Congress approves the reform that gives full powers to Ortega and his wife

author: time news
title: The Nicaraguan Congress approves the reform that gives full powers to Ortega and his wife
text:
The‚ĀĘ Nicaraguan Congress approved on Friday a constitutional reform that guarantees President Daniel Ortega and his wife Rosario‚ÄĆ Murillo, as ‚Äúco-president‚ÄĚ, absolute control of the state‚Äôs powers.
The National (Legislative) Assembly, ‚Äčcontrolled by the‚Äč ruling Sandinista ‚Ā§National Liberation Front‚Ā§ (FSLN, ‚ÄĆleft), ‚Äúunanimously‚ÄĚ approved the initiative presented by the‚Äć president on Tuesday, parliamentary ‚Äćleader Gustavo Porras announced before the session plenary.
The controversial reform extends the‚Ā£ presidential mandate ‚Äčfrom five to six years and confirms ‚Äčthe power Murillo already has by equaling ‚Ā§that of Ortega,‚Äč as it‚Äč raises ‚Äćhis rank from vice president to co-president.
The text attributes total powers to‚ĀĘ the Executive, as it establishes that the co-presidents will coordinate ‚Äúthe legislative, judicial, electoral, control and supervisory, regional ‚Ā£and municipal bodies‚ÄĚ, which the Constitution already‚ĀĘ recognized as independent.
Ortega, a ‚ÄĆ79-year-old former guerrilla who‚Äč ruled Nicaragua in the 1980s after the triumph of‚Ā£ the‚ĀĘ Sandinista revolution, returned to power ‚Ā§in‚ÄĆ 2007. Since then, his critics say, he has established a ‚Äúdictatorship‚ÄĚ‚Ā£ and ‚Äúnepotism‚ÄĚ with‚Ā£ his‚Äč wife., six years younger.
Both have radicalized their positions and increased control over Nicaraguan society after the ‚Äć2018‚ĀĘ protests, whose repression caused ‚Ā£320 deaths according to the UN,‚Äč considered by‚Äč the ‚Äćgovernment to be an attempted coup sponsored‚Ā£ by Washington.
‚ÄúRevolutionary‚ÄĚ state.
The reform,‚Äč which was approved ‚ÄĆin the‚Äč first mandate and will be approved in the ‚Äćsecond mandate in January‚ÄĆ ‚Äď Porras‚Ā£ acknowledged ‚Äď has unleashed ‚Äća‚Ā§ wave of rejection on the part of Nicaraguans in exile ‚Ā§and international organizations, which the United States and‚Äč the United States have joined. ‚ÄĆ‚ÄôOrganization of‚Äč American ‚Ā£States. ‚ĀĘ(OAS).
In the reform,‚Äč Nicaragua‚Ā£ is defined as a ‚Äúrevolutionary‚ÄĚ and socialist state, and includes ‚Äćamong its‚Äć national symbols‚Ā§ the red and black ‚Ā£flag of the FSLN, a former guerrilla under whose leadership a popular uprising ‚ĀĘoverthrew the dictator Anastasio‚ÄĆ Somoza in 1979.
For ‚ĀĘAzah√°lea‚Ā§ Sol√≠s, an expert in constitutional law, this leaves out political projects‚Ā§ of‚ĀĘ other ‚Äćideologies, and Salvador Marenco, ‚Äća human rights lawyer exiled in Costa ‚Ā£Rica, assured ‚Ā§that it ‚ÄĆis the‚Äć end ‚Ā£of ‚Ā£the separation of powers and political ‚Äćpluralism in Nicaragua.
‚ÄúAll that is now ‚Ā§in ‚Äčthe reform is what, in fact, is happening in Nicaragua: a de ‚Äčfacto dictatorship. The‚Äč novelty is that it will now ‚Äčbe in the Constitution,‚ÄĚ said former guerrilla commander Dora Mar√≠a T√©llez, who she ‚Ā§was‚ĀĘ incarcerated. and lives in exile in the United States.
Social control
The reform‚ÄĆ establishes social‚Äč control not only of ‚ĀĘthe ‚ĀĘpress, the Church and economic‚ĀĘ entities, but‚ÄĆ also ‚Ā£formalizes the withdrawal of Nicaraguan nationality from those ‚ÄĆconsidered ‚Äútraitors of the homeland‚ÄĚ, as the government did with‚ĀĘ approximately 450 critics and opponents.
During the massive protests of 2018, ‚ĀĘhooded and heavily armed men intervened to set ‚ĀĘup trenches set ‚Ā§up by university students and control‚Äć the‚Ā§ demonstrators. ‚ÄćThousands of people went into exile.
In‚Ā§ the reform, the government proposes‚ÄĆ the creation of a ‚Äúvoluntary police‚ÄĚ, made‚Ā§ up of civilians, as an ‚Äúauxiliary and support body‚ÄĚ to the security forces.
What impact will the recent constitutional reform have on ‚ÄćNicaragua‚Äôs democratic institutions and civil liberties? Interview ‚ÄćBetween Time.news Editor and Political Expert Dr. Elena Rivas
Time.news Editor : Welcome, Dr. Rivas. Thank you for joining us today‚Ā§ to discuss the recent constitutional ‚Ā£reform in Nicaragua. The Nicaraguan Congress has approved changes that grant President Daniel Ortega and his wife,‚Ā§ Rosario Murillo, significant powers. How do you interpret this move within the context of Nicaraguan‚Ā£ politics?
Dr. Elena Rivas : Thank ‚Ā§you for having me. This constitutional‚Ā£ reform‚Ā£ is‚Äč a significant escalation in Ortega‚Äôs consolidation of power. By elevating Murillo to the role of co-president and extending the presidential ‚Äčmandate from five to six years, ‚ĀĘit allows them to exert‚Ā£ almost unchecked control over all branches of government. This move reflects a clear ‚Äćintent to cement a‚ÄĆ dynastic regime and effectively eliminate any checks on executive power.
Time.news Editor : It‚Äôs alarming, to say the least. The reform was approved unanimously in a legislature‚Äč controlled by ‚ÄćOrtega‚Äôs party, the Sandinista National Liberation Front (FSLN). What does this‚Ā£ tell us about the state of democracy in‚Ā£ Nicaragua today?
Dr. Elena Rivas : The unanimous approval is quite telling‚ÄĆ and further ‚Äćexemplifies the erosion of democratic principles in Nicaragua. ‚Ā£Essentially, the ‚ĀĘlegislative‚Äč assembly has transformed into a rubber stamp‚ĀĘ for Ortega‚Äôs agenda. There‚Äôs a troubling trend of centralizing‚Äć power that‚ÄĆ not only undermines democratic institutions but also places Nicaragua ‚Äćfirmly in the realm of authoritarian governance. The opposition has been systematically ‚Äćsuppressed, leaving little room ‚Ā£for ‚Äčdissent.
Time.news Editor : The reforms‚ÄĆ also redefine ‚Äćthe state‚Äč as ‚Äúrevolutionary‚ÄĚ and socialist, reinforcing the legitimacy of Ortega‚Äôs government. How might this‚ÄĆ affect Nicaragua‚Äôs international standing?
Dr. Elena Rivas : By defining Nicaragua as a ‚Äúrevolutionary‚ÄĚ and ‚Äúsocialist‚ÄĚ state, Ortega is attempting to ‚Ā£frame his ‚Ā§rule in historical terms and rally domestic support.‚Äć However, this stance‚Ā£ is likely to strain relations‚Äč with international organizations, including the U.S. and the OAS, particularly as they have condemned the reform. Nicaragua risks becoming increasingly isolated, facing potential sanctions or other‚ÄĆ diplomatic repercussions, which would further complicate its socio-economic situation.
Time.news Editor : Indeed, considering the backdrop of the 2018 protests and‚Äč the government‚Äôs subsequent crackdown, where over 320 ‚Äćdeaths were reported. How do you think ‚Ā£this reform will impact civil society and dissenting voices?
Dr. Elena Rivas : The implications for civil society are ‚Äčdire. Given the context‚ĀĘ of the 2018 protests, where dissent was met with extreme repression, the constitutional changes will likely stifle any remnants of opposition. Activists, journalists,‚Äć and ordinary‚ÄĆ citizens who challenge the government will face even greater risks, leading ‚Ā£to a more oppressive environment. Those in exile are ‚Äčalready voicing their rejection, and it‚Äôs crucial for‚Ā£ the international community to support ‚Äćthem while advocating for human rights in Nicaragua.
Time.news Editor : It sounds like ‚Ā§a ‚Äćprecarious situation for many Nicaraguans. As an expert, what measures would you recommend for the international‚ÄĆ community ‚Äčto take?
Dr. Elena Rivas :‚Ā£ The‚Äć international community must adopt a multi-faceted approach. First, ‚ĀĘcontinue to monitor human rights violations and hold the Ortega regime accountable through sanctions and diplomatic ‚Ā§pressure. Second,‚Äč support Nicaraguan civil society organizations dedicated to human rights and democratization.‚Ā£ Lastly, engaging in dialog with regional partners to foster ‚Ā£a collective‚ÄĆ response can amplify the call for restoring democracy and human rights in Nicaragua.
Time.news Editor : Thank you, Dr. Rivas, for‚ÄĆ your ‚Ā£insights on this complicated and critical issue. Your analysis sheds light on‚Äć the considerable challenges Nicaragua faces as it navigates this ‚ĀĘperiod‚Ā£ of uncertainty.
Dr. Elena Rivas : ‚Ā§Thank you for having me. It is important to keep the conversation going; the situation in Nicaragua deserves global attention.
